content.full_text:
Fern PolrrrcRl Pnecrrcss CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
December 29,2OIO
Richard Breitwieser
P.O. Box 321
Diablo, California 94528
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-188
Dear Mr. Breitwieser:
This letter responds to your request on behalf of the directors of the Diablo Community
Service District (the "District") for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. the Fair political
Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Do the members of the Diablo Community Service District Board of Directors have
conflicts of interest that prohibit them from participating in governmental decisions to approve
funding for a traffic study to evaluate the traffic impact of a proposed development planned for
construction across the street from the District? The members all own real property within the
District, but none of that real property is within 500 feet of the proposed development.
CONCLUSION
No. Because the members of the Diablo Community Service District Board of Directors
own real property that would be indirectly involved in the governmental decisions that will be
before them, they will not have conflicts of interest when participating in those decisions so long
as there are Ilo factors present to rebut the presumption that the decision will not have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the director's economic interest in his
or her real property. (See Regulation 18705.2(b).) Because the governmental decision is to
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulaticlns of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitornia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-188
Page No. 2
approve or disapprove of funds for the traffic study, there are no factors present that would rebut
the presumption of nonmateriality.2
FACTS
You are the General Manager of the Diablo Community Service District ("District").
The District's Board of Directors ("Board") has authorized you to seek advice on their behalf.
The Board is composed of five members, each of whom is a home owner within the boundaries
of the District. A new housing development has been proposed across the street from the
District. The houses in the new development will be smaller and on much smaller lots than the
homes within the District. If the development goes through and the smaller homes are built on
the smaller lots, the property values within the District may depreciate. The development will
also put more cars on the roads that service the District and those additional cars will impact
District residents and may also depreciate values of property within the District. Because each
Board Member is also a home owner within the District, it is possible that their individual
property values will depreciate if the new development is approved. You state that development
of the project would affect each member's real property in the jurisdiction in the same manner
and none of their properties are within 500 feet of the possible new development.
Residents of the District have requested that the Board take a position adverse to the new
development and spend District funds to study the impact of the new development on the District
and to possibly oppose the new development. The question is whether the possible depreciation
in value of property owned by each Board Member within the District, as a result of the
development, is such a financial impact that it creates a conflict of interest that would prohibit
the Board members from voting on a resolution to provide District funds to study the impact of
the new development on the District community or to oppose the development.
ANALYSIS
PorBNrnL CoNFLrcr oF INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
2 In a telephone conversation with Senior Commission Counsel Bill Lenkeit on December 28, 2010, you
indicated that at the present time the issue was limited to providing funds for a traffic study but, that as some later
point, there may or may not be an issue to provide funds to hire an attorney to fight the implementation of the
housing development. If that issue does develop later on, you are encouraged to write again for f urther advice with
respect to that issue.


File No. A-10-188
Page No. 3
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.
srBps I &2t AnB THp BoaRn Mrnrnnns punuc orrrcnr,s MAKTNG,
PanrrcplrrNc rN MAKTNG, oR INFLUENCTNG A GovERNMENTAL DpctsroN?
The Members of the Board are public officials under the Act. (Section 82048.)
Consequently, they may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use their official positions
to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any
of their economic interests. Because they will be called upon to consider approval of funding to
study and possibly oppose the new development, they will be making, participating in making, or
otherwise using their official positions to influence governmental decisions.
Srrp 3: Do THr Bolno MBunBns H.lvn PorrNrmr,Ly DrseuALrFyrNG EcoNovrrc
INrBnBsrs?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regularion 1g703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 19703.2.)
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to rhe decision. (section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
a
a
a
a
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)


File No. A-I0-188
Page No.4
You have stated that the Board Members own homes in the jurisdiction, and they have at
least a $2,000 interest in their homes. You have not provided any information with respect to
any other economic interests. Accordingly, our analysis is limited to their economic interests in
their homes.
Srnp 4: Is Tun EcoNotnc INrBnnsr Dmrcrr,y oR INDTRECTLy lNvor,vno rN THE
GovrnNuENTAL DBcrsroN?3
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision."
(Regulation 1870a(a).) For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the
standards set forth in Regulation 18704.2 apply. Because the real property owned by each of the
Board Members does not fall into any of the criteria listed in Regulati on 18704.2 (copy
enclosed), their real property is indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
Srrp 5: MlrnRrlr,my SrlNolno
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)
Regulation I 8705.2(b)(l) provides
"The financial effect of a govemmental decision on real property that is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material."
(Regulation 18705.2(b).) This presumption may be rebutred by proof rhat there
are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial
effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the real property in which the public official
has a interest. Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
" (B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
" (C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial
effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or
similar traits of the neighborhood. "
I If a public official's economic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is
considered "indirectly involved." (Regulation 1870a(a).)


File No. A-10-188
Page No. 5
Accordingly, because the facts you have provided indicate there are no factors present to
rebut the presumption under Regulation 18705.2(b). the financial effect of the governmental
decisions on the Board Members' economic interests is not material, and they would not be
prohibited from participating in the governmental decisions to provide funds for a traffic study
related to the potential new development.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division
SKB jgl
Enclosure

sections.question:
Do the members of the Diablo Community Service District Board of Directors have
conflicts of interest that prohibit them from participating in governmental decisions to approve
funding for a traffic study to evaluate the traffic impact of a proposed development planned for
construction across the street from the District? The members all own real property within the
District, but none of that real property is within 500 feet of the proposed development.
sections.conclusion:
No. Because the members of the Diablo Community Service District Board of Directors
own real property that would be indirectly involved in the governmental decisions that will be
before them, they will not have conflicts of interest when participating in those decisions so long
as there are Ilo factors present to rebut the presumption that the decision will not have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the director's economic interest in his
or her real property. (See Regulation 18705.2(b).) Because the governmental decision is to
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained inPage No. 2
approve or disapprove of funds for the traffic study, there are no factors present that would rebut
the presumption of nonmateriality.2

CONCLUSION: No. Because the members of the Diablo Community Service District Board of Directors
